title: Pork vindaloo
subtitle: Goan pork curry in a vinegar and chilli paste
excerpt: Pork shoulder marinated and braised in a wet paste of dried chillies, vinegar, garlic, and warm spices. The Goan curry that crossed into Anglo-Indian cooking and became Britain's benchmark for heat, properly made, it is sour and deeply spiced rather than merely hot.
sourceNotes: Based on the Goan vindaloo tradition documented in Floyd Cardoz, 'Floyd Cardoz: Flavorwalla' (2016) and Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005). Vindaloo descends from the Portuguese carne de vinha d'alhos (meat in wine and garlic), transformed by Goan cooks who substituted palm vinegar for wine and added the chillies that arrived with the Portuguese in the sixteenth century.

content.text: A properly made vindaloo is not a competition in heat. The central flavour is vinegar, tart and mouth-puckering, balanced against warm spices: cumin, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves. The dried chillies provide intensity and colour, but the dish should be sour first. This is the tradition of Goa, where palm vinegar was the souring agent before Portuguese contact and the carne de vinha d'alhos evolved into something entirely new.

The paste is made by grinding dried chillies, garlic, and spices with red wine vinegar (a good stand-in for the original palm vinegar) into a thick, brick-red paste. The pork sits in this for at least two hours, then braises covered with a little water until the meat is tender and the sauce has reduced to coat it.

items.name: Chilli flakes
items.prepNote: or 4 to 6 dried red chillies, deseeded

content.text: Toast {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds, {{cinnamon-stick}} broken cinnamon stick, the seeds from {{cardamom-pods}} cardamom pods, {{cloves}} cloves, and {{black-pepper}} whole peppercorns in a dry pan for 2 minutes. Grind to a powder. Blend the ground spices with {{garlic}} garlic cloves, {{ginger-fresh}} ginger, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli, {{turmeric-ground}} of turmeric, and {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar to a smooth paste. Toss {{pork-shoulder}} of pork shoulder cubes in the paste with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours.

Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} sliced onions and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add the marinated pork and all the paste. Stir and cook for 5 minutes until the pork is lightly browned. Add 200 ml of water, bring to the boil, cover tightly, and reduce to a low simmer. Cook for 70 to 80 minutes until the pork is completely tender and the sauce is thick. Check and stir every 20 minutes, adding a small splash of water if the sauce catches. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.fix: Start with 1 teaspoon of chilli flakes and taste the paste before adding the pork. Serve with plain yoghurt alongside to cut the heat.
items.cause: Too many chillies or very potent dried chillies
items.symptom: Vindaloo is too hot to eat

items.fix: A long braise naturally softens the vinegar's edge. If still sharp after 70 minutes, add a small pinch of sugar and cook for another 10 minutes.
items.cause: Too much vinegar, or not enough cooking time to mellow
items.symptom: Sauce is acidic to the point of sharpness

items.fix: Shoulder needs a proper simmer to break down. Increase the heat slightly, add a splash of water, and continue for another 20 minutes.
items.cause: Heat too low, or pork cut too large
items.symptom: Pork is still chewy at 80 minutes

content.text: Vindaloo is one of the clearest examples of colonial food exchange. A Portuguese keeping method (meat in wine and garlic to last at sea) was taken up by Goan cooks, spiced with ingredients the Portuguese had themselves brought to India, and sent back to Europe as something else entirely. British troops in Goa came across it in the nineteenth century. It travelled home with Anglo-Indian families and later with the first wave of Goan migrants to Britain. By the 1970s it had become a marker of British curry-house heat, a reputation that has overshadowed its first character as a sour and layered braise.
